title: John Carmack on how high-bandwdith flash can power AI inference
gist: John Carmack argues NAND flash—over 100x cheaper per GB than HBM—can serve AI inference because model inference has a deterministic, sequential memory-access pattern that tolerates millisecond cold-start latency, enabling much larger and cheaper models via high-bandwidth flash (HBF), though training remains a poor fit due to flash write-wear.

themes.theme: Flash economics vs HBM for inference

insights.claim: NAND flash is over 100 times cheaper per GB than HBM, creating an opportunity for AI accelerators even after giving a flash controller a 1024-bit interface with HBM-class bandwidth.
insights.speaker: S
insights.so_what: A 100x cost gap is a large enough wedge to reshape inference memory hierarchies—bullish for NAND makers and a potential threat to pure-HBM demand growth.
insights.quote: NAND flash is over 100 times cheaper per GB than HBM, so there should be opportunity there, even after giving a flash controller a 1024 bit interface with HBM bandwidth.
insights.locator: NAND flash is over 100 times cheaper

insights.claim: Model inference has a deterministic memory-access pattern that needs no true random access for weights and can tolerate multi-millisecond cold-start latency as long as continuous reads deliver the necessary bandwidth.
insights.speaker: S
insights.so_what: Determinism is the technical key unlocking cheap sequential flash for weights—the core enabler of the HBF-for-inference thesis.
insights.quote: Unlike game rendering, model inference can have a deterministic memory access pattern. You don’t need “random access memory” at all for model weights, and you could tolerate cold-start latencies in the multiple milliseconds
insights.locator: Memory cost and capacity are significant issues

insights.claim: Carmack proposes either a specialized pin protocol pipelining full 16KB+ pages from flash to accelerator scratchpad, or emulating random-access memory with a 1000x+ performance cliff for non-sequential reads that reuses existing cache hierarchies and eases model-weight updates.
insights.speaker: S
insights.so_what: The RAM-emulation path lowers adoption risk by working immediately (slowly) before optimization—an incremental deployment route chipmakers could favor.
insights.quote: the ram-emulation interface will start off just extremely slow, and you can incrementally sort out the changes for full performance
insights.locator: You could make a specialized pin protocol

insights.claim: When scratchpad SRAM is too small to hold a layer's weights, Carmack suggests reviving the old optical-drive trick of duplicating data at multiple sequential locations to avoid seeking, noting there would be capacity to burn.
insights.speaker: S
insights.so_what: Flash's cheap capacity permits redundancy-for-performance tradeoffs impossible with expensive HBM—an architectural degree of freedom.
insights.quote: the old optical drive optimization technique of duplicating data in multiple places on a sequential read to avoid seeking, but there would be capacity to burn
insights.locator: There may be cases where there isn’t enough scratchpad SRAM

insights.claim: A split memory system mixing flash and HBM channels would likely be suboptimal versus uniform memory but could be much cheaper and allow much larger models to run.
insights.speaker: S
insights.so_what: Hybrid flash/HBM designs point to a new accelerator memory tier; watch for controller and interface IP opportunities.
insights.quote: A split memory system consisting of some channels of flash and some channels of HBM will probably be suboptimal compared to a uniform memory, but it could be much cheaper, and allow much larger models to be run.
insights.locator: A split memory system consisting of some channels

insights.claim: Carmack argues the case is strong for inference but weak for training, because flash memory would quickly wear out from the writes even if perfectly page-aligned.
insights.speaker: S
insights.so_what: Bounds the HBF opportunity to inference; training memory demand still favors HBM/DRAM, limiting the disruption's scope.
insights.quote: flash memory would quickly wear out from the writes, even if they were all perfectly page aligned
insights.locator: I think th case is strong for inference

insights.claim: For training, Carmack suggests replacing low-latency HBM with massively parallel cheaper DRAM at high latency could still yield worthwhile cost savings.
insights.speaker: S
insights.so_what: Even in training, a cost-driven shift toward high-latency parallel DRAM could pressure HBM's premium positioning.
insights.quote: Replacing low-latency HBM with massively parallel cheap(er) DRAM at high latency might still be a worthwhile cost savings.
insights.locator: I think th case is strong for inference
